O-01.name: Supply chain concentration & disruption

supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.source: erp_supply_chain.csv — TSMC critical-path supplier (18-week lead time)
supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.description: Inventory cover weeks for the critical-path single-source supplier (TSMC, 18-week lead time). Amber threshold 8w = TSMC target_inventory_weeks from ERP. Breach threshold 4w = minimum safe operating level (~22% of TSMC lead time). Current 5.2w = amber: 2.8 weeks below target, 12.8 weeks below TSMC lead time — a TSMC disruption today results in production stop in 5.2 weeks with no substitution path.
supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.threshold_rationale: TSMC 18-week lead time benchmark: amber at target inventory (8w), breach at 22% of lead time (4w)

O-01.agent_findings:
OPERATIONAL RISK ASSESSMENT — 2026-05-28

O-01 SUPPLY CHAIN — BREACH (multiple KRIs)

The supply chain is in a structurally vulnerable position across three intersecting dimensions: concentration, inventory, and supplier financial health.

Concentration: 62.6% of $12.61B total spend flows through single-source suppliers — 25.6 percentage points above the 50% breach threshold. The three single-source relationships are TSMC (Semiconductors-CPU, $4.2B, Taiwan), Foxconn (Manufacturing-Assembly, $2.1B, China), and Quanta (ODM-Laptops, $1.6B, Taiwan). These three suppliers alone represent $7.9B of addressable spend with zero substitution path available in the near term. Geographic concentration compounds this: 53.5% of spend is Taiwan-domiciled (TSMC and Quanta), meaning a single geopolitical or natural disaster event in Taiwan creates an unhedged exposure across the two largest single-source relationships simultaneously.

Inventory: Minimum cover stands at 3.9 weeks, breaching the 4-week floor. This figure is set by Quanta at 3.9 weeks. Against Quanta's 6-week lead time, the organisation is already below the replenishment cycle. Against TSMC's 18-week lead time, a supply interruption today would exhaust CPU inventory within the month and could not be remediated for approximately 5 months per the stress model's recovery_months estimate. The stress model quantifies revenue at risk at $9.1B and supply chain VaR (95th percentile) at $2,634M. The cost saving from achieving dual-sourcing is modelled at $382M and from inventory buffering at $305M — neither is currently being captured.

Supplier financial health: Two suppliers carry distress scores below 65. Yangtze Memory [SUP008] at score 55 is the weaker of the two but is a multi-source supplier, partially mitigating its individual impact. More critically, Quanta [SUP008, score 61] is a single-source supplier with 3.9 weeks of inventory — this combination simultaneously triggers the escalation rule (single-source + financial_health < 65 + inventory < 4 weeks). Quanta's OTD of 88.7% and rejection rate of 1.2% are the weakest performance metrics of any named single-source supplier, suggesting operational strain consistent with its financial stress score. A Quanta failure or significant capacity reduction would remove the ODM-Laptops production path with less than one replenishment cycle of buffer.

ESCOPED EBITDA IMPLICATION: The 24% probability of demand shock (demand_shock_prob_pct) carrying a 15% impact (demand_shock_impact_pct) against a $9.1B revenue exposure implies an expected value of demand shock revenue loss of approximately $327M. The dual-sourcing programme, if executed, would structurally reduce VaR exposure by $382M — a remediation programme with a positive expected return that is not yet active.

---

O-02 CYBER AND IT — BREACH (multiple KRIs)

The cyber posture presents multiple simultaneous control failures that together create a materially elevated incident probability and an extended undetected exposure window.

Detection and response: MTTD is 11 days, breaching the 10-day threshold. Mean time to respond is 18 days. The combined detection-plus-response window of up to 29 days means that an active intrusion or data exfiltration event could persist for nearly a month before containment. With 4 security incidents recorded month-to-date, this is not a theoretical risk — active security events are occurring within an environment where detection is slow.

Patch compliance: At 71%, patch compliance breaches the 80% floor by 9 percentage points. With 8 critical vulnerabilities open beyond 30 days (amber, approaching the 10-count breach threshold) and 23 high vulnerabilities in the same state, the unpatched attack surface is substantial. The 45 days of unreviewed privileged access is a direct pathway for lateral movement or privilege escalation in the event of a credential compromise — a risk elevated by phishing attempt rate of 2.3% and MFA coverage gaps (87%, amber).

RTO: The order management system RTO of 6 hours is at the breach boundary of 6 hours. Any system outage affecting OMS directly impairs the organisation's ability to process and fulfil orders, translating cyber disruption into immediate revenue impact. The revenue quantum is not specified in the data provided.

Supplier cyber resilience: The supplier cyber resilience assessment completion rate is 0%. This means that for all suppliers — including the three single-source critical path suppliers TSMC, Foxconn, and Quanta — there is no assessed understanding of their cyber security posture. This is an escalation trigger condition given that active security incidents (4 MTD) are occurring in the same period. A supply chain cyber attack (e.g. a compromise of Foxconn's manufacturing execution systems) would be undetected from a supplier-side assessment perspective, and with MTTD already at 11 days internally, cross-boundary detection would likely be slower still.

Control effectiveness note: Multiple KRIs in breach with no active remediation programme cited in the data, and control indicators (patch rate, MTTD, privileged access review lag) all pointing away from compliance thresholds. This domain is assessed as deteriorating.

---

O-03 PRODUCT QUALITY — AMBER (no safety incidents confirmed)

Product quality presents no confirmed safety incidents (safety_incidents_ytd = 0, regulatory_product_notices_ytd = 0) — the zero-tolerance escalation threshold is not triggered. This is the primary positive signal in the operational risk profile this period.

However, three amber conditions warrant attention:

Recall readiness: Score of 72, sitting 8 points above the 70-point breach threshold and below the 80-point amber threshold. Critically, the last recall simulation was run 8 months ago. The organisation cannot currently validate that its recall execution capability matches its documented readiness score. If a safety event were to occur — for example driven by the deteriorating quality metrics at Quanta (1.2% rejection rate) or Foxconn (0.8% rejection rate) — the response capability would be untested against current processes and personnel.

Supplier quality rejection rate: 0.68%, amber (threshold 0.5%, breach at 1.0%). The 1.2% rejection rate at Quanta specifically is already above the breach threshold for supplier quality rejection. This is masked at the aggregate level but represents a live quality risk in the ODM-Laptops production line. Quanta's financial distress (score 61) is a leading indicator that quality investment may decline further.

Warranty claims: 1,842 claims month-to-date. The EBITDA cost of this volume is not quantified in the data provided, but it represents a direct margin drag and, if the rate is rising, a potential signal of field failure rate deterioration ahead of the metric itself moving.

Field failure rate at 0.019% and first pass yield at 97.2% remain within acceptable parameters. The quality domain is not in systemic failure, but the recall readiness gap and Quanta-specific rejection rate require active monitoring given the supply concentration risk.

---

O-04 TALENT — BREACH (multiple KRIs, ESCALATION TRIGGERED)

Talent risk has crossed escalation thresholds and represents an active operational continuity risk in two critical functions.

Flight risk concentration — R&D: Three flight-risk employees are flagged within R&D, a single critical function. This triggers the escalation rule. R&D is the function responsible for product pipeline integrity, supplier qualification support, and long-term quality architecture. The simultaneous loss of three flagged individuals in this function would create an immediate knowledge gap with compounding effects on product quality (O-03), the pace of dual-sourcing qualification (O-01 remediation), and potentially on security tool development and vulnerability management (O-02).

Critical open roles: 7 roles have been open beyond 60 days across the organisation (breach threshold: 6). In Engineering specifically, average time to fill a critical role is 68 days and the compensation gap versus market is 8.2%. With a counter-offer acceptance rate of only 42% in Engineering, the organisation is losing more than half of the candidates it makes offers to in its most capacity-constrained function. This is a structural hiring problem, not a transient pipeline issue.

Attrition: Engineering attrition at 14.2% is amber (threshold 12%), IT-Operations at 11.8% is approaching the amber threshold. The IT-Operations attrition figure is particularly significant given the concurrent O-02 control failures — patch compliance at 71% and MTTD at 11 days are both consistent with a chronically understaffed security operations function.

Succession: SVP succession plan coverage at 72% is amber (threshold 80%). With only 72% of SVP-level roles covered by a succession plan and engagement at 67%, there is a meaningful probability that a leadership departure in an uncovered SVP role would create a governance and decision-making gap at a time when the organisation faces material supply chain and cyber remediation decisions.

EBITDA implication: The cost of the 8.2% compensation gap in Engineering is not quantified in the data provided, but closing this gap — the most direct lever to reduce attrition and counter-offer loss rate — will carry a payroll cost increase. The alternative cost of continued open roles (lost productivity, recruitment fees, onboarding) is also unquantified but material given 7 roles open beyond 60 days.

---

CROSS-DOMAIN SUMMARY

The four operational risk domains are not independent this period. The supply chain's dependence on three financially or operationally stressed single-source suppliers (O-01) is unmitigated by either cyber visibility into those suppliers (O-02, 0% resilience assessment) or by the workforce capacity to execute a dual-sourcing remediation programme (O-04, 7 Engineering roles unfilled). Quanta's quality deterioration (O-03, 1.2% rejection rate) is directly linked to its financial distress (O-01) and reduces confidence in the ODM-Laptops production line precisely when inventory cover is thinnest. The organisation's ability to detect and respond to a supply chain cyber event is constrained by both MTTD (11 days) and IT-Operations attrition. These interactions mean that the residual risk in any single domain understates the aggregate operational exposure.

O-02.name: Cyber attack & IT resilience

cyber_supply_response_gap_days.description: Days by which combined MTTD+MTTR cyber response time exceeds available inventory cover. Positive value = production halt guaranteed if cyber incident disrupts supplier comms. Amber: 0 (response just meets inventory); Breach: >0 (response exceeds inventory). Target: ≤ -2 days (2-day safety margin).

Justification: Likelihood score of 2 is inconsistent with active breach conditions — warranty_claims_mtd at 1,842 (up from 1,612), recall_readiness at 72 (amber, 2 points from breach), recall simulation last run 8 months ago. Upstream supply quality (Quanta rejection rate 1.2%, Foxconn 0.8%) is a confirmed leading driver of downstream field defects. Per O-04 override precedent: forward-looking indicators and breach trajectory take precedence over lagging control effectiveness score. CRO sign-off required next review cycle.

Against TSMC's 18-week lead time, a supply interruption today would exhaust CPU inventory within the month and could not be remediated for approximately 5 months per the stress model's recovery_months estimate. The stress model quantifies revenue at risk at $9.1B and supply chain VaR (95th percentile) at $2,634M. The cost saving from achieving dual-sourcing is modelled at $382M and from inventory buffering at $305M — neither is currently being captured.

Supplier financial health: Two suppliers carry distress scores below 65. Yangtze Memory [SUP008] at score 55 is the weaker of the two but is a multi-source supplier, partially mitigating its individual impact. More critically, Quanta [SUP008, score 61] is a single-source supplier with 3.9 weeks of inventory — this combination simultaneously triggers the escalation rule (single-source + financial_health < 65 + inventory < 4 weeks). Quanta's OTD of 88.7% and rejection rate of 1.2% are the weakest performance metrics of any named single-source supplier, suggesting operational strain consistent with its financial stress score. A Quanta failure or significant capacity reduction would remove the ODM-Laptops production path with less than one replenishment cycle of buffer.

ESCOPED EBITDA IMPLICATION: The 24% probability of demand shock (demand_shock_prob_pct) carrying a 15% impact (demand_shock_impact_pct) against a $9.1B revenue exposure implies an expected value of demand shock revenue loss of approximately $327M. The dual-sourcing programme, if executed, would structurally reduce VaR exposure by $382M — a remediation programme with a positive expected return that is not yet active.

---

O-02 CYBER AND IT — BREACH (multiple KRIs)

The cyber posture presents multiple simultaneous control failures that together create a materially elevated incident probability and an extended undetected exposure window.

Detection and response: MTTD is 11 days, breaching the 10-day threshold. Mean time to respond is 18 days. The combined detection-plus-response window of up to 29 days means that an active intrusion or data exfiltration event could persist for nearly a month before containment. With 4 security incidents recorded month-to-date, this is not a theoretical risk — active security events are occurring within an environment where detection is slow.

Patch compliance: At 71%, patch compliance breaches the 80% floor by 9 percentage points. With 8 critical vulnerabilities open beyond 30 days (amber, approaching the 10-count breach threshold) and 23 high vulnerabilities in the same state, the unpatched attack surface is substantial. The 45 days of unreviewed privileged access is a direct pathway for lateral movement or privilege escalation in the event of a credential compromise — a risk elevated by phishing attempt rate of 2.3% and MFA coverage gaps (87%, amber).

RTO: The order management system RTO of 6 hours is at the breach boundary of 6 hours. Any system outage affecting OMS directly impairs the organisation's ability to process and fulfil orders, translating cyber disruption into immediate revenue impact. The revenue quantum is not specified in the data provided.

Supplier cyber resilience: The supplier cyber resilience assessment completion rate is 0%. This means that for all suppliers — including the three single-source critical path suppliers TSMC, Foxconn, and Quanta — there is no assessed understanding of their cyber security posture. This is an escalation trigger condition given that active security incidents (4 MTD) are occurring in the same period. A supply chain cyber attack (e.g. a compromise of Foxconn's manufacturing execution systems) would be undetected from a supplier-side assessment perspective, and with MTTD already at 11 days internally, cross-boundary detection would likely be slower still.

Control effectiveness note: Multiple KRIs in breach with no active remediation programme cited in the data, and control indicators (patch rate, MTTD, privileged access review lag) all pointing away from compliance thresholds. This domain is assessed as deteriorating.

---

O-03 PRODUCT QUALITY — AMBER (no safety incidents confirmed)

Product quality presents no confirmed safety incidents (safety_incidents_ytd = 0, regulatory_product_notices_ytd = 0) — the zero-tolerance escalation threshold is not triggered. This is the primary positive signal in the operational risk profile this period.

However, three amber conditions warrant attention:

Recall readiness: Score of 72, sitting 8 points above the 70-point breach threshold and below the 80-point amber threshold. Critically, the last recall simulation was run 8 months ago. The organisation cannot currently validate that its recall execution capability matches its documented readiness score. If a safety event were to occur — for example driven by the deteriorating quality metrics at Quanta (1.2% rejection rate) or Foxconn (0.8% rejection rate) — the response capability would be untested against current processes and personnel.

Supplier quality rejection rate: 0.68%, amber (threshold 0.5%, breach at 1.0%). The 1.2% rejection rate at Quanta specifically is already above the breach threshold for supplier quality rejection. This is masked at the aggregate level but represents a live quality risk in the ODM-Laptops production line. Quanta's financial distress (score 61) is a leading indicator that quality investment may decline further.

Warranty claims: 1,842 claims month-to-date. The EBITDA cost of this volume is not quantified in the data provided, but it represents a direct margin drag and, if the rate is rising, a potential signal of field failure rate deterioration ahead of the metric itself moving.

Field failure rate at 0.019% and first pass yield at 97.2% remain within acceptable parameters. The quality domain is not in systemic failure, but the recall readiness gap and Quanta-specific rejection rate require active monitoring given the supply concentration risk.

---

O-04 TALENT — BREACH (multiple KRIs, ESCALATION TRIGGERED)

Talent risk has crossed escalation thresholds and represents an active operational continuity risk in two critical functions.

Flight risk concentration — R&D: Three flight-risk employees are flagged within R&D, a single critical function. This triggers the escalation rule. R&D is the function responsible for product pipeline integrity, supplier qualification support, and long-term quality architecture. The simultaneous loss of three flagged individuals in this function would create an immediate knowledge gap with compounding effects on product quality (O-03), the pace of dual-sourcing qualification (O-01 remediation), and potentially on security tool development and vulnerability management (O-02).

Critical open roles: 7 roles have been open beyond 60 days across the organisation (breach threshold: 6). In Engineering specifically, average time to fill a critical role is 68 days and the compensation gap versus market is 8.2%. With a counter-offer acceptance rate of only 42% in Engineering, the organisation is losing more than half of the candidates it makes offers to in its most capacity-constrained function. This is a structural hiring problem, not a transient pipeline issue.

Attrition: Engineering attrition at 14.2% is amber (threshold 12%), IT-Operations at 11.8% is approaching the amber threshold. The IT-Operations attrition figure is particularly significant given the concurrent O-02 control failures — patch compliance at 71% and MTTD at 11 days are both consistent with a chronically understaffed security operations function.

Succession: SVP succession plan coverage at 72% is amber (threshold 80%). With only 72% of SVP-level roles covered by a succession plan and engagement at 67%, there is a meaningful probability that a leadership departure in an uncovered SVP role would create a governance and decision-making gap at a time when the organisation faces material supply chain and cyber remediation decisions.

EBITDA implication: The cost of the 8.2% compensation gap in Engineering is not quantified in the data provided, but closing this gap — the most direct lever to reduce attrition and counter-offer loss rate — will carry a payroll cost increase. The alternative cost of continued open roles (lost productivity, recruitment fees, onboarding) is also unquantified but material given 7 roles open beyond 60 days.

---

CROSS-DOMAIN SUMMARY

The four operational risk domains are not independent this period. The supply chain's dependence on three financially or operationally stressed single-source suppliers (O-01) is unmitigated by either cyber visibility into those suppliers (O-02, 0% resilience assessment) or by the workforce capacity to execute a dual-sourcing remediation programme (O-04, 7 Engineering roles unfilled). Quanta's quality deterioration (O-03, 1.2% rejection rate) is directly linked to its financial distress (O-01) and reduces confidence in the ODM-Laptops production line precisely when inventory cover is thinnest. The organisation's ability to detect and respond to a supply chain cyber event is constrained by both MTTD (11 days) and IT-Operations attrition. These interactions mean that the residual risk in any single domain understates the aggregate operational exposure.

appetite_thresholds.domain_statement: Low appetite for supply chain disruption and product quality failures. Zero tolerance for major safety incidents. Low tolerance for cyber incidents: MTTD must stay below 5 days; patch compliance must exceed 95%.

# Strategic Risk Assessment — Current Run

---

## 1. DOMAIN STATUS
The strategic risk portfolio is in a **critical posture**: S-01 has breached its KRI threshold driven by concentrated geopolitical pressure, while S-02 carries an amber warning on AI competitive disruption, and S-03 remains the sole domain holding within acceptable parameters.

---

## 2. S-01 FINDING — Geopolitical Risk | **BREACH**
Five geopolitical signals were detected this run, of which four are rated high severity. The signals **"US expands Entity List by 14 technology companies," "Taiwan Strait military exercises increase in frequency,"** and **"EU announces reciprocal tariff review on Chinese electronics"** collectively indicate simultaneous supply-chain, trade, and military-posture stress across three distinct theatres. The concentration of four high-severity signals in a single domain is the primary driver of the KRI breach status.

---

## 3. S-02 FINDING — AI Disruption Risk | **AMBER**
Three competitive signals were detected: **"Dell launches AI-native laptop line with on-device LLM," "HP announces partnership with Google for Gemini-embedded Chromebook Pro,"** and **"India announces PLI scheme expansion for domestic PC manufacturing."** The first two signals indicate accelerating AI-native product deployment by direct competitors, compressing the window for a differentiated response. The third signal introduces an emerging manufacturing-cost dynamic in a high-growth market.

---

## 4. S-03 FINDING — M&A Integration Risk | **OK**
Three active deals are in the pipeline: **CloudScale Systems** (Integration-Month 18, USD 1,200M), **NeuralEdge AI** (Due Diligence, USD 450M), and **DataVault Security** (Negotiation, USD 280M). Synergy delivery is currently tracking at **84.0%** against target, with CloudScale representing the largest capital exposure and the most advanced integration stage. The KRI is within bounds, though the 16-point gap to full synergy target on the portfolio's largest deal warrants continued monitoring.

---

## 5. TOP ACTIONS

| Risk | Action |
|---|---|
| **S-01** | Convene an emergency supply-chain war-gaming session to map Entity List exposure across tier-1 and tier-2 suppliers and activate contingency sourcing protocols before further escalation. |
| **S-02** | Accelerate the AI product roadmap review to assess competitive gap against Dell and HP-Google offerings and establish a clear go/no-go decision point for on-device AI integration. |
| **S-03** | Conduct a targeted synergy recovery review on **CloudScale Systems** to identify the root cause of the gap to the 100% target and assign ownership for close-out actions before Month 24. |

---

## 6. ESCALATION
**YES** — S-01 KRI status is **BREACH**. Immediate escalation to senior leadership and the Board Risk Committee is required.

F-01.agent_findings:
## Financial Risk Assessment — 2026-05-28

### F-01: FX & Commodity Exposure

**FX Portfolio (primary KRI scope per CF-04 2026-06-07)**
Total gross FX exposure across four currency pairs (USD_CNY, USD_EUR, USD_JPY, USD_KRW) is USD 9,600M on a combined basis. Applying position-level hedges, unhedged FX exposure stands at USD 4,080M against an amber threshold of USD 4,000M and breach threshold of USD 5,000M. The portfolio average hedge ratio is 46.3%, which is below the 60% amber trigger and materially above the 45% breach threshold by only 1.3 percentage points — a narrow buffer. FX unrealised P&L is USD -26.6M.

By position:
- **FX001 USD_CNY (Revenue, JPMorgan)**: Gross USD 3,200M, hedged USD 1,400M (43.8%), unhedged USD 1,800M, unrealised P&L USD -8.4M, maturity 6 months. Largest single unhedged position; hedge ratio below both amber and approaching breach threshold.
- **FX002 USD_EUR (Revenue, Deutsche Bank)**: Gross USD 2,800M, hedged USD 1,200M (42.9%), unhedged USD 1,600M, unrealised P&L +USD 7.2M, maturity 12 months. Only position with positive unrealised P&L; hedge ratio below amber threshold.
- **FX003 USD_JPY (Cost, HSBC)**: Gross USD 980M, hedged USD 600M (61.2%), unhedged USD 380M, unrealised P&L USD -15.6M, maturity 3 months. Only position with hedge ratio above 60% amber threshold; however, 3-month maturity creates near-term re-hedging requirement.
- **FX004 USD_KRW (Cost, Citibank)**: Gross USD 620M, hedged USD 320M (51.6%), unhedged USD 300M, unrealised P&L USD -9.8M, maturity 6 months.

**Commodity Portfolio (supplementary context)**
COM001 DRAM (Goldman Sachs): gross USD 890M, hedged USD 400M (44.9%), unrealised P&L USD -12.3M, maturity 3 months. COM002 NAND (JPMorgan): gross USD 650M, hedged USD 280M (43.1%), unrealised P&L USD -8.1M, maturity 3 months. Combined commodity unrealised P&L is USD -20.4M, flowing through COGS into gross profit and EBITDA.

**Combined unrealised P&L for covenant stress**: FX USD -26.6M (operating income channel) + Commodity USD -20.4M (COGS channel) = USD -47.0M total portfolio mark-to-market. This is relevant to COV001 EBITDA headroom analysis — see F-03 section.

**Trend assessment**: Amber on both unhedged exposure and hedge ratio KRIs. Hedge ratios for three of four FX positions (USD_CNY 43.8%, USD_EUR 42.9%, USD_KRW 51.6%) are below the amber trigger, with USD_CNY and USD_EUR approaching the 45% breach threshold. No data provided on active hedging programme or CFO-approved hedge ratio recovery plan; trend assessed as deteriorating.

---

### F-02: Revenue Concentration & AR Quality

**Top-Customer Concentration**
Enterprise Tier-1 top-customer concentration is 22.1%, above the 20% amber threshold and below the 25% breach threshold. Headroom to breach is 2.9 percentage points. Government Tier-1 shows 14.2% concentration, within appetite. All other segments (Tier-2 8.4%, Tier-3 2.1%, Consumer 1.2%) are within tolerance.

**Bad Debt Provision — BREACH**
Portfolio bad debt provision is 1.44%, in confirmed breach of both the F-02 KRI amber threshold (0.40%) and breach threshold (0.55%), and in confirmed breach of COV006 Trade-Finance covenant threshold (0.80%). Segment analysis:
- SMB Tier-3: USD 32.1M provision, 90-day overdue USD 22M — highest absolute provision per revenue segment relative to size.
- Enterprise Tier-2: USD 28.6M provision, 90-day overdue USD 18M.
- Consumer Tier-4: USD 18.4M provision, 90-day overdue USD 12M.
- Enterprise Tier-1: USD 12.4M provision, 90-day overdue USD 8M — lowest relative provision, consistent with credit quality of named accounts.
- Government Tier-1: USD 2.8M provision, 90-day overdue USD 0M.

Total 90-day overdue balance is USD 60.0M across all segments. The SMB and Consumer segments are the primary drivers of provision deterioration.

**Trend assessment**: Bad debt KRI is in breach and the provision rate has worsened from 1.10% (prior test COV006) to 1.44% (current), confirming a deteriorating trend. Concentration KRI is flat at amber.

---

### F-03: Liquidity & Refinancing

**COV006 — CONFIRMED ACTIVE BREACH (Trade-Finance Facility)**
COV006 bad debt provision at 1.44% exceeds the 0.80% threshold by 64 basis points. This is an existing breach — not a forward probability — and requires immediate cure or waiver action.

Cure deadline: **2026-06-12**.

Cure scenarios and EBITDA impact:
- Full write-off scenario: USD 94.3M flows through P&L, consuming 62% of USD 152M COV001 EBITDA headroom, leaving approximately USD 58M before COV001 breach on RCF-USD2B.
- Partial write-off scenario (USD 50M): EBITDA headroom falls to approximately USD 102M.

**MANDATORY EXECUTIVE ACTION — LENDER NOTIFICATION**:
(a) BOARD DIRECTIVE REQUIRED — lender notification is a board-authorised act under credit facility terms.
(b) Written board authorisation must be obtained before the Group Treasurer contacts lenders.
(c) Hard deadline for board resolution: **2026-06-12**.
(d) Named joint owners: Group Treasurer AND CFO; CFO co-signature required per facility terms.
(e) Cross-default risk arises if the notification obligation is not met before the 2026-06-30 test date.

**COV001 — Net Debt/EBITDA (RCF-USD2B) — Amber, Deteriorating**
Current ratio 2.8x vs threshold 3.0x; headroom 0.2x ratio units = USD 152M EBITDA headroom (derived: Net Debt USD 6.384B / 3.0x threshold = USD 2.128B EBITDA floor; current EBITDA USD 2.28B less floor USD 2.128B = USD 152M). This has deteriorated from 2.6x (headroom 0.4x) at the 2026-03-31 test. Total portfolio unrealised P&L of USD -47.0M (FX USD -26.6M + commodity USD -20.4M) represents 30.9% of USD 152M EBITDA headroom if fully realised. COV006 full cure write-off would consume a further 62%, leaving only approximately USD 58M headroom.

**COV002 — Interest Coverage (Term-Loan-A) — OK, Deteriorating**
Current 4.2x vs threshold 3.5x; headroom 0.7x. Declined from 1.3x at prior test, a 0.6x reduction quarter-on-quarter. At the current rate of deterioration this covenant will reach amber within two quarters absent corrective action.

**COV003 — Liquidity Headroom (RCF-USD2B) — OK**
Current USD 1.1B vs threshold USD 0.8B; headroom USD 0.3B. Declined from USD 1.2B / USD 0.4B headroom at prior test. Remains above the USD 0.9B escalation trigger for total liquidity (current USD 1.1B), but the downward trend warrants monitoring. Escalation trigger would activate at USD 0.9B.

**COV004 — Debt Maturity Runway (Bond-2027) — Amber**
Current 8 months vs threshold 12 months; headroom 0 months against covenant. The escalation trigger activates at 6 months, providing 2 months of buffer before mandatory escalation. Runway has compressed from 11 months at the 2026-03-31 test to 8 months at the 2026-06-30 test. No active refinancing programme is referenced in the data; trend is deteriorating.

**COV005 — EBITDA Floor (All-Facilities) — OK**
Current USD 2.28B vs floor USD 1.80B; headroom USD 0.48B. Declined from USD 2.51B / USD 0.71B headroom at prior test (USD 230M EBITDA erosion quarter-on-quarter). The COV006 cure scenarios (USD 50M–USD 94.3M write-off) would further reduce this headroom to USD 385M–USD 433M under COV005 terms, while simultaneously tightening COV001 as described above.

**Trend assessment**: F-03 residual risk is materially elevated. COV006 is in confirmed breach. COV001 and COV004 are amber with deteriorating trajectories and no evidence of deleveraging or refinancing programmes in the data.

---

### F-04: Accounting & Reporting

The AUD003 Internal-SOX review of Financial Controls has completed with 1 high finding and 0 critical findings, and 0 material weaknesses identified. The open high finding count of 1 is below the amber threshold of 2 and well below the breach threshold of 5. No escalation trigger applies (material weakness count = 0).

**Trend assessment**: Flat. Single high finding does not indicate systemic control failure. No material weakness escalation required. The finding should be tracked to remediation closure to prevent recurrence.

---

### Cross-Domain Interconnection Summary

1. **F-02 → F-03 COV006 cascade**: The bad debt provision deterioration from 1.10% to 1.44% is the direct cause of the COV006 breach. The cure cost (USD 50M–USD 94.3M) simultaneously threatens COV001 headroom on the largest facility (RCF-USD2B), creating a two-covenant compression scenario from a single underlying credit quality issue.

2. **F-03 + O-01**: EBITDA has eroded by USD 230M quarter-on-quarter to USD 2.28B. Any supply chain disruption that further compresses EBITDA — via commodity cost increases flowing through COGS, or FX losses flowing through operating income — acts against a USD 152M COV001 buffer that is already being pressured by the COV006 cure requirement. The combined unrealised mark-to-market of USD -47.0M (USD -26.6M FX + USD -20.4M commodity) is directly relevant to this stress scenario.

3. **F-01 + S-01**: USD_CNY (USD 1,800M unhedged) and USD_KRW (USD 300M unhedged) together account for USD 2,100M — 51.5% — of total unhedged FX exposure in geopolitically flagged currency pairs. The Strategic Risk Agent should assess S-01 implications for these positions.

4. **F-02 + S-03**: Enterprise Tier-1 top-customer concentration at 22.1% amber. M&A pipeline changes affecting the Enterprise Tier-1 customer base could accelerate this metric toward or away from the 25% breach threshold. Cross-flag to Strategic Risk Agent for S-03 pipeline review.

### C-03 — ANTI-BRIBERY & ESG

**Third-Party ABAC Coverage (third_party_abac_coverage_pct = 89%) — BREACH**
The current coverage of 89% is below the breach threshold of <95% under the FCPA (regulatory_horizon.csv: FCPA, effective 1977-12-19, gap: 'Third-party coverage at 89% vs 100%', owner: CCO) and UK Bribery Act (regulatory_horizon.csv: UK-Bribery-Act, effective 2011-07-01, gap: 'Third-party coverage at 89% vs 100%', owner: CCO). This is an active breach. Critically, AUD001 (Third-Party-ABAC Tier-1 Suppliers) has completed with 3 findings including 2 high findings — a significant escalation from the prior period where AUD001 showed 0 findings. The CCO must immediately review and remediate these 2 high findings from the Tier-1 ABAC audit. AUD002 (Third-Party-ABAC Tier-2 Distributors) is In-Progress with a due date of 2026-06-30. The trend is improving (85%→89%) but remains in breach and a 6-percentage-point gap to the 95% threshold remains. No confirmed FCPA or Bribery Act violation is recorded in the provided data. The breach reflects a control gap, not a confirmed violation; however, in zero-tolerance domains, the absence of confirmed violation does NOT diminish the obligation to achieve 100% coverage.

**Whistleblower Cases (whistleblower_high_findings = 1)**
AUD006 (Whistleblower-Cases All-Regions) records 4 total findings including 1 high finding (up from 3 findings/1 high in the prior period). The escalation trigger for C-03 requires that a whistleblower case involves an SVP-level or above individual. The audit data does NOT specify the seniority level of individuals involved in the high finding. This is a data gap: the absence of seniority information means the SVP-level escalation trigger cannot be definitively confirmed or ruled out. Confidence on this finding is therefore LOW. The CCO (audit owner) must confirm whether any of the 4 open whistleblower cases, and specifically the 1 high finding, involves an SVP-level or above individual. If confirmed, immediate escalation is required.

**CSRD Scope 3 Disclosure (csrd_scope3_disclosure_pct = 74%)**
regulatory_horizon.csv records CSRD (EU, effective 2024-01-01, gap: 'Scope 3 disclosure at 74% vs 80% target', owner: CSO). The current disclosure rate of 74% is below the 80% target. The KRI threshold note states this is 'FYI context only — no KRI threshold' for breach/amber determination; no formal breach is recorded against this KRI. AUD007 (ESG-Disclosure Scope3-Emissions) is In-Progress, due 2026-09-30. The trend is improving (70%→74%). The CSO must ensure the 80% target is met before the AUD007 due date. The gap between the CSRD effective date (2024-01-01) and the current disclosure rate constitutes an ongoing partial compliance position under EU CSRD that the Board should note.

board_summary:
RISK POSTURE

The organisation carries 17 KRI breaches and 18 amber warnings across all four risk domains simultaneously, representing a systemic risk condition. Three or more domains are concurrently in breach, triggering the systemic risk flag and requiring immediate board-level attention.

STRATEGIC: The strategic domain records 1 breach and 2 amber warnings, with the Chief Executive Officer accountable. KRI S-01 has breached its threshold driven by 5 geopolitical signals, prompting formal escalation; S-02 carries an amber on AI competitive disruption, and S-03 remains an active watch item. The compound scenario S-01+F-01 flags that 5 geopolitical signals coincide with elevated FX exposure, warranting urgent review of the hedging position. The compound scenario S-01+F-01 further identifies USD 2,100M of total unhedged FX exposure concentrated in USD/CNY and USD/KRW, currency pairs directly associated with geopolitically flagged jurisdictions; escalation to the Strategic Risk Agent is recommended. The compound scenario F-02+S-03 notes that Enterprise Tier-1 top-customer concentration of 22.1% sits in amber territory, and three active M&A deals — CloudScale Systems, NeuralEdge AI, and DataVault Security — could materially shift customer revenue concentration toward or beyond the 25% breach threshold.

OPERATIONAL: The operational domain records 10 breaches and 7 amber warnings, with accountability distributed across the COO, CISO, and CHRO by sub-domain. The supply chain is structurally vulnerable across concentration, inventory, and supplier financial health dimensions, with 62.6% of USD 12.61B spend flowing through single-source suppliers and minimum inventory at only 3.9 weeks. This is a data/config change, not a board-text change. Add the following row to kri_thresholds.csv:

risk_id,kri_name,amber_threshold,breach_threshold,direction,unit,description
O-02,supplier_cyber_resilience_assess_pct,50,50,lower_worse,%,Percentage of suppliers with completed cyber resilience assessment completed against NIST framework or equivalent

Alternatively, if O-01 is intended to be primary owner with O-02 as cross-reference, add to O-01 and document cross-reference in description. The compound scenario O-01+O-03 identifies Quanta [SUP005] as carrying both a rejection rate of 1.2% approaching the 1.0% breach threshold and a financial distress score of 61; deterioration in Quanta's quality controls would directly elevate warranty and field failure exposure under O-03 (field_failure_rate_pct currently PROVISIONAL pending QMS-SAP reconciliation due 14 June 2026). The compound scenario O-02+O-04 connects IT-Operations attrition of 11.8% and 7 critical roles open beyond 60 days to patch compliance at 71% and mean time to detect of 11 days, consistent with an understaffed security function. The compound scenario O-03+O-04 flags three flight-risk employees in R&D as a leading indicator for future field failure rate increases and supplier qualification gaps, both currently in amber territory. The compound scenario O-01+O-04 notes that Engineering attrition of 14.2% and 7 unfilled critical Engineering roles directly impede Append to the supply chain operational risk narrative (before distribution to board): 'The dual-sourcing programme USD 382M saving is derived from: Rev-at-risk USD 9,100M × annual disruption probability [X%] × recovery factor [Y] = USD 382M benefit. Implementation cost estimated at USD 14.1M (implying 27× ROI). Model assumptions: TSMC lead time 18 weeks; dual-source diversion cost premium 3–8% per supplier; inventory buffer increase to 6–8 weeks minimum. These assumptions should be validated by the COO/VP Supply Chain before board approval.'.

FINANCIAL: The financial domain records 4 breaches and 7 amber warnings, with the Chief Financial Officer accountable. EBITDA has declined from USD 2.51B to USD 2.28B quarter-on-quarter, a reduction of USD 230M, compressing COV001 Net Debt/EBITDA headroom on RCF-USD2B to USD 152M (USD 57.7M effective post-COV006 cure). The compound scenario F-03+O-01 warns that combined mark-to-market stress of USD 47.0M — comprising commodity unrealised P&L of USD -20.4M and FX unrealised P&L of USD -26.6M — sits directly against that USD 152M buffer, and any supply chain disruption flowing through COGS risks triggering a COV001 breach. The compound scenario F-02+F-03 identifies a cascade from the bad debt provision breach at 1.44% to a COV006 breach on the Trade-Finance facility; a full write-off cure of USD 94.3M would reduce EBITDA headroom from USD 152M to approximately USD 58M, pushing COV001 to near-breach on RCF-USD2B. Total gross FX exposure across USD/CNY, USD/EUR, USD/JPY, and USD/KRW is USD 9,600M, with USD 4,080M unhedged on a net basis; USD 2,100M of that unhedged position is concentrated in geopolitically flagged currency pairs. The compound scenario F-02+S-03 further notes that Enterprise Tier-1 top-customer concentration at 22.1% amber, combined with active M&A pipeline activity across three named deals, could shift the metric materially relative to the 25% breach threshold, and cross-referral to the Strategic Risk Agent is recommended.

COMPLIANCE: The compliance domain records 2 breaches and 2 amber warnings, with the Chief Compliance Officer and General Counsel jointly accountable. Active breaches are AI audit coverage at 78% against the less-than-90% threshold under the EU AI Act obligation effective 2026-08-02, and third-party ABAC coverage, which remains incomplete under AUD001 and AUD002 closing 2026-06-30. The compound scenario involving denied party matches flags that three pending resolutions — up from two in the prior period — could constitute compound exposure if any match is associated with a Tier-1 or Tier-2 supplier also subject to the incomplete ABAC programme, creating simultaneous restricted-party engagement and inadequate anti-bribery due diligence risk on the same counterparty; the CCO must cross-reference denied party matches against the open Tier-2 ABAC population before AUD002 closes. A further compound scenario notes that two export licences expiring within 30 days — ECC-2024-0047 covering TSMC components and ECC-2024-0061 covering DRAM modules — involve systems that may also process personal data of end-users or counterparty personnel; if licence lapse occurs and shipment continues, trade-compliance system logs containing personal data could constitute a GDPR data processing gap, and the Operational Agent for O-02 should be notified if lapse affects data-processing systems.

KEY RISK DRIVERS

The most consequential combination of findings this period is the simultaneous convergence of a confirmed covenant breach, a critically degraded cyber posture, and a supply chain carrying insufficient inventory buffer — all three interacting in a way that amplifies the financial consequence of either of the other two failing. COV006 is confirmed in active breach today: the bad debt provision of 1.44% exceeds both the internal management action trigger of 0.55% and the COV006 lender covenant threshold of 0.80%. The Chief Financial Officer must notify the lending syndicate and present a cure strategy to the Board by 12 June 2026. The cure requires a full provision of USD 94.3M, which reduces COV001 gross headroom from USD 152.0M to an effective post-cure figure of USD 57.7M. When the combined USD 47.0M mark-to-market portfolio losses — USD 26.6M from FX positions and USD 20.4M from commodity positions — are applied against that post-cure headroom, the combined worst-case COV001 headroom narrows to USD 10.7M. Separately, the probability of an additional COV001 Net Debt/EBITDA breach at the 30 June 2026 test date is assessed at 50.5% — meaning the organisation is more likely than not to breach a second covenant at the next test — though this figure is PROVISIONAL pending a full model rerun due by 20 June 2026 and must be labelled as such in all board materials. Cross-default risk is flagged as a confirmed binary breach, meaning failure to cure COV006 before the test date could trigger acceleration across the broader facility suite. This financial fragility is not self-contained: the five geopolitical signals in breach — three of which are rated high severity, covering the US Entity List expansion, Taiwan Strait military activity, and EU reciprocal tariff review — create timing pressure on both the hedging programme and the supply chain. With USD 2,100M of the USD 4,080M unhedged FX exposure concentrated in currency pairs directly associated with geopolitically flagged jurisdictions, and with the FX hedge ratio at 46.3% against a 60% policy target and the commodity hedge ratio at 44.2% in confirmed breach of its 45% threshold, the organisation is carrying material unhedged market risk precisely when geopolitical volatility is at its highest this period. The cyber posture compounds this further: with mean time to detect at 11 days, patch compliance at 71%, MFA coverage at 87%, privileged access unreviewed for 45 days, and IT recovery time objective at its 6-hour limit, the organisation's ability to contain a cyber incident in the current threat environment is materially impaired. The board should treat the intersection of these three domains — financial covenant stress, geopolitical and market risk, and cyber control failure — as the primary risk cluster requiring executive decision this period.

CROSS-DOMAIN CONNECTIONS

The most material cross-domain interaction this period is the chain running from talent loss through cyber control failure to supply chain vulnerability, with the financial covenants as the ultimate receptor of all three. Domain agents have established that IT-Operations attrition and seven critical roles open beyond 60 days are a direct contributing factor to patch compliance standing at 71% and mean time to detect reaching 11 days — the organisation's cyber controls are degrading because the teams responsible for maintaining them are understaffed. This matters far beyond an IT control failure: the supplier cyber resilience assessment rate is at 0%, meaning that for all suppliers — including the three single-source critical-path suppliers whose combined spend reaches USD 7.9B — there is no assessed understanding of their security posture. With only 3.9 weeks of inventory cover, a cyber incident originating from or targeting any unassessed single-source supplier could simultaneously trigger a supply disruption and a data or IP breach, with no advance warning and no buffer to absorb the shock. The financial consequence of that scenario is direct and severe: supply chain disruption flowing through cost of goods sold would further compress EBITDA against a COV001 headroom that is already reduced to USD 57.7M post-cure and as low as USD 10.7M in the combined worst case. The same talent constraint that is weakening cyber controls is also impeding the dual-sourcing programme — the primary identified mitigation against supply chain concentration risk, with a modelled saving of USD 382M — because engineering attrition at 14.2% and seven unfilled critical engineering roles reduce the organisation's capacity to qualify alternative suppliers. A second material connection runs from geopolitical signals to FX hedging to covenant stress: three of the five geopolitical signals in breach directly affect the jurisdictions associated with USD 2,100M of unhedged FX exposure, and any further deterioration in those positions would apply additional pressure to a COV001 headroom that has almost no remaining margin for error in the worst-case scenario. Additionally, with cyber resilience and talent simultaneously flagged across O-02 and O-04, the board should note that flight-risk staff in revenue-critical and security-critical functions — combined with a mean time to detect of 11 days and a recovery time objective at its limit — creates conditions in which an active incident could persist and cause revenue loss for nearly a month before containment, a consequence that extends well beyond an IT control failure into direct trading impact. Finally, the three active M&A deals in pipeline, with two in pre-close stages, carry the potential to shift Enterprise Tier-1 customer concentration, which is already at amber at 22.1%, and the compliance domain's three pending denied party matches, if any involve a Tier-1 or Tier-2 supplier also subject to incomplete ABAC coverage, would constitute a compound restricted-party and anti-bribery exposure that the Chief Compliance Officer must resolve before the AUD002 close date of 30 June 2026.

QUARTER-ON-QUARTER MOVEMENT

Aggregate residual risk has risen materially from the prior quarter. Of 20 KRIs compared, 15 are deteriorating in trend direction, three are improving, and two are stable — a ratio that confirms broad-based worsening across the portfolio. The dominant cause is INHERENT RISK GROWTH: the underlying exposures themselves have expanded rather than controls simply failing to keep pace. The geopolitical signal count moved from two signals to five, crossing into breach. Inventory cover fell from 4.3 weeks to 3.9 weeks, breaching the 4.0-week floor. The bad debt provision rose from 1.24% to 1.44%, remaining in breach and deepening it. The net debt to EBITDA ratio moved from 2.6x to 2.8x, narrowing COV001 headroom. These are all expansions of the underlying risk exposure. Control weakening is also present — patch compliance fell from 78% to 71% and the supplier cyber resilience assessment rate fell from 15% to 0% — but these are secondary to the inherent risk growth driving the aggregate movement. The primary decision point the board can act on to reduce residual risk the most this period is mean time to detect, which moved from 9 days to 11 days and crossed into breach. Restoring detection capability through emergency cyber programme resourcing, directly linked to filling the seven critical open roles that are the identified root cause, would reduce the organisation's exposure window across both the cyber and supply chain dimensions simultaneously.

RISK COMMITTEE RECOMMENDED ACTIONS:
  • Direct the CFO to notify the lending syndicate of the COV006 covenant breach immediately and present a formal cure strategy to the Board by 12 June 2026 (action_deadline per KRI data). The Committee must be informed that COV006 is confirmed in active breach today with certainty — this is separate from, and in addition to, the 50.5% PROVISIONAL probability of an additional COV001 breach at the 30 June 2026 test date. The cure provision of USD 94.3M reduces effective COV001 headroom from gross USD 152.0M to USD 57.7M post-cure; applying the USD 47.0M combined mark-to-market portfolio losses against that post-cure figure leaves a combined worst-case COV001 headroom of USD 10.7M. Inaction by 12 June 2026 risks cross-default activation, which is currently flagged as a confirmed binary breach.
  • Require the CISO to present an emergency remediation plan to the Committee within 10 business days addressing all six concurrent cyber control failures in O-02: mean time to detect at 11 days (threshold 10), patch compliance at 71% (threshold 80%), IT recovery time objective at its 6-hour limit, MFA coverage at 87% (threshold 90%), privileged access unreviewed at its 45-day limit, and supplier cyber resilience assessments at 0% (threshold 50%). The plan must specifically address the compound risk identified by domain agents — that 0% supplier assessment combined with 62.6% single-source concentration and only 3.9 weeks inventory cover means a supplier-originated cyber incident could trigger simultaneous supply disruption and data or IP breach with no advance warning. The Committee must be told that seven critical roles open beyond 60 days are a direct contributing factor to the patch compliance and detection failures, and the CHRO must confirm a hiring timeline for those roles as part of the same submission.
  • Direct the CFO / Group Treasurer to present a hedge ratio remediation plan at the next Committee meeting covering: (a) an immediate plan to restore the commodity hedge ratio above the 44.2% breach level to at least the 45% threshold; (b) a roadmap to close the FX hedge ratio from 46.3% toward the 60% policy target; and (c) a specific review of the USD 2,100M unhedged FX exposure concentrated in geopolitically flagged currency pairs given the geopolitical signal count breach at 5 (threshold 3). The combined stress-scenario loss range of USD 755M to USD 1,057M across FX and commodity positions, set against the effective post-cure covenant headroom of USD 57.7M, illustrates that unhedged market losses could threaten covenant compliance independent of the EBITDA trajectory.
  • Require the Chief Operating Officer, VP Supply Chain, and CHRO to deliver a joint dual-sourcing and supplier resilience plan to the Committee within 30 days, addressing: single-source concentration at 62.6% (threshold 50%); geographic concentration at 75.3% (threshold 75%); inventory cover at 3.9 weeks (threshold 4.0 weeks, deteriorating); and the 0% supplier cyber resilience assessment rate. The plan must quantify progress toward the USD 382M dual-sourcing saving identified by the stress model and must explicitly address how the engineering talent constraint — 14.2% attrition and 7 open critical engineering roles — will be resolved, given that talent gaps directly impede the supplier qualification work required to execute that mitigation.
  • Direct the Chief Compliance Officer and General Counsel to report to the Committee before the next scheduled meeting on three compliance exposures: (a) resolution timeline for the three pending denied party matches, with cross-reference to the open Tier-2 ABAC population before the AUD002 close date of 30 June 2026, given that a denied party match against an entity also subject to incomplete ABAC coverage constitutes a compound restricted-party and anti-bribery exposure; (b) renewal status of the two export licences expiring within 30 days (ECC-2024-0047 and ECC-2024-0061 covering semiconductor and memory components), with confirmation that shipment will be suspended if renewal is not confirmed before expiry; and (c) a trajectory for closing AI audit coverage from 78% to the 90% threshold and third-party ABAC coverage from 89% to the 95% threshold, both of which remain in breach despite prior-quarter improvement.

cov001_board_narrative.text: IMPORTANT — COV001 AMBER RATIO VS FORWARD BREACH PROBABILITY: The Net Debt to EBITDA ratio currently reads 2.8× (amber — below the 3.0× breach ceiling). This ratio reflects the position as at the Q2 reporting date and is correct. However, the EBITDA Stress Monte Carlo separately estimates a 50.5% probability that this ratio will exceed 3.0× (model_params authoritative MC value using effective headroom USD 57.7M — 2026-06-09; original figure 69.4% was a calculation error corrected 2026-06-09) by the 2026-06-30 test date — a breach-status forward indicator. These two signals are not contradictory: the ratio measures where the organisation is today; the Monte Carlo measures where it is likely to be in 21 days under plausible EBITDA scenarios. Both signals must be read together. The amber ratio does NOT indicate the covenant position is under control — the forward probability of 50.5% means more likely than not that a COV001 breach will occur at the next test date. The board should treat this as a breach-trajectory situation requiring the same urgency as an active breach. A deterministic scenario in which the COV006 provision charge of USD 94.3M (corrected; prior USD 365M was a calculation error) recognised in full would reduce EBITDA to approximately USD 2,185.7M — USD 57.7M above the COV001 implied floor of USD 2,128M — meaning the cure alone does not deterministically breach COV001, but the Monte Carlo shows P(breach) = 50.5% under plausible EBITDA scenarios at test date.
